turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion this morning in Case 22-1074, Sheetz versus County of El Dorado.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: George Sheetz and his wife applied for a permit to build a modest, prefabricated home on their rural property with plans to raise their grandson there. But before the County of El Dorado would issue the building permit, it demanded that Sheetz pay a substantial fee to mitigate local traffic congestion. Sheetz challenged the fee in California State Court as an unlawful exaction of money in violation of the Fifth Amendment’s Takings Clause. His challenge relied on this Court’s decisions in Nollan versus California Coastal Commission and Dolan versus City of Tigard. In those cases, we held that permit conditions must have an essential nexus to the government’s land use interest, and the cost of complying with the condition must be roughly proportional to the harms that will flow from the proposed development. Sheetz argued that the county’s demand for the traffic fee failed the Nollan-Dolan test because the fee amount was determined by a generic rate schedule rather than an individualized assessment of how Sheetz’s modest new home might affect local traffic. But the lower courts declined to assess the fee under Nollan and Dolan’s nexus and rough proportionality test. In their view, and deepening a split among other courts to consider this issue, the county’s traffic fee need not satisfy Nollan and Dolan because the permit condition was imposed by the Legislature and not on an ad hoc basis by an administrative agency. The California Court was wrong. Nothing in the Constitution’s text, history, or this Court’s precedent supports treating legislative and administrative permit conditions differently for purposes of the Takings Clause. Start with the text. Neither the Fifth Amendment, which contains the Takings Clause, nor the Fourteenth Amendment, which incorporates the Takings Clause against the states, distinguishes among the different branches of government. As far as the constitutional text is concerned, legislative and administrative permit conditions stand on equal footing. History points the same way. In fact, special deference for legislative takings would have made little sense historically because legislation was the typical way that governments exercised their eminent domain power. Before, during, and after the Founding, legislatures passed laws to acquire private land for a variety of public uses, and they always provided just compensation to the landowners. The Takings Clause enshrines that longstanding practice. This Court’s precedent seals the deal. In both our per se and regulatory Takings cases, we have subjected legislative and administrative action to the same constitutional rules. So, too, for the many circumstances in which we have applied the unconstitutional conditions doctrine, there is no reason to treat the Nollan-Dolan test any differently. This case contains many issues that we don’t decide, including whether a permit condition imposed on a class of properties must be tailored with the same degree of specificity as one that targets a particular development. The California Court did not consider this issue, or several others, because it proceeded from the erroneous premise that legislatively imposed permit conditions are categorically exempt from the rules of Nollan and Dolan. We leave the unaddressed issues for the lower courts to consider in the first instance. We vacate the judgment below and remand the case for further proceedings not inconsistent with this opinion. Our opinion is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion, which Justice Jackson has joined. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion, and Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion, which Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson have joined.
